# Font palettes

<FeatureLinks mdn="https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/search?q=%E5%AD%97%E4%BD%93%E8%B0%83%E8%89%B2%E6%9D%BF%20CSS" caniuse="https://caniuse.com/?search=%40font-palette-values" status="Fonts 4/5" label="Reference links" />

## Plain-language explanation

Theme and customize color font palettes.

## Representative syntax

```css
font-palette, @font-palette-values
```

## Demo

<CssDemo title="Font palettes" css-b64="" code-b64="LyogRmVhdHVyZSBmb2N1czogZm9udC1wYWxldHRlLCBAZm9udC1wYWxldHRlLXZhbHVlcyAqLwouZGVtby1jYXJkIHsKICBtYXgtaW5saW5lLXNpemU6IDU2MHB4Owp9Ci5kZW1vLXRpdGxlIHsKICB0ZXh0LXdyYXA6IGJhbGFuY2U7Cn0KLmRlbW8tY29weSB7CiAgbWF4LWlubGluZS1zaXplOiA0NGNoOwogIGh5cGhlbnM6IGF1dG87Cn0=" html-b64="" caption="This example uses realistic heading and summary copy to show how typography features improve a reading surface." lang="en-US" badge="Practical demo" />

## When to use it

<div class="usage-grid">
<section>
  <h3>Best fit</h3>
  <p>Use Font palettes in theme and state systems when you need to build color variations from semantic inputs instead of hand-tuning every state. It usually gives the stylesheet a clearer job than extra classes, wrappers, or script.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Example</h3>
  <p>A warning banner can derive its background, border, and text color from one token and still adapt in dark mode.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Watch out</h3>
  <p>Perceptual spaces help with ramps, but they do not replace contrast checks against real text sizes and backgrounds.</p>
</section>
</div>

- Representative syntax: `font-palette, @font-palette-values`
